topics: ⚖️ The split list: liable for his ox, exempt for himself — and vice versa
👨‍👩‍👦 Striking parents: bull liable, person exempt (capital crime)
🔥 Setting fire on Shabbat: נִידּוֹן בְּנַפְשׁוֹ exempts from money
💸 Ben Pedaya: כָּל הַמְקַלְקְלִין פְּטוּרִין — destroyers are exempt except מבעיר and one who wounds
🐂 Serial gorer: the last victim is paid first (הָאַחֲרוֹן אַחֲרוֹן נִשְׂכָּר)

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Asymmetry Between Person and Ox
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The Mishnah teases out cases where a person and his ox produce opposite liabilities — sometimes the ox triggers payment while the owner himself is exempt, and sometimes the reverse. The unifying principle behind the exemption pattern is קַם לֵיהּ בְּדְרַבָּה מִינֵּיהּ — when the act carries a capital penalty, monetary liability falls away. Ben Pedaya then frames the broader principle: spoilers (מְקַלְקְלִין) on Shabbat are exempt except the מבעיר and the one who wounds. The chapter then turns to the serial gorer.
overview.hebrewSource: יֵשׁ חַיָיב עַל מַעֲשֵׂה שׁוֹרוֹ וּפָטוּר עַל מַעֲשֵׂה עַצְמוֹ. פָּטוּר עַל מַעֲשֵׂה שׁוֹרוֹ וּחַיָיב עַל מַעֲשֵׂה עַצְמוֹ

slides.title: ⚖️ Owner Liable / Owner Exempt — The Mishnah's Inversions
slides.subtitle: Five paired scenarios revealing the asymmetry.
rows: Exempt — no בושת for animal damage
Exempt — capital offense subsumes the money
קַם לֵיהּ בְּדְרַבָּה מִינֵּיהּ blocks the money

callout.label: The pattern:
callout.text: An animal cannot be tried capitally as a moral agent in these cases. So pure damages still apply. A human, however, when the same act carries death or other supreme penalty, escapes the lesser monetary obligation.

slides.title: 🔥 Why Person Is Exempt Where Ox Is Liable
slides.subtitle: Three different exemption mechanisms working in parallel.

boxes.title: 👨‍👩‍👦 Striking Parent
items: Capital crime if blood is drawn
Money obligation absorbed by death penalty
Bull, not subject to that law, just pays

boxes.title: 🔥 Burning on Shabbat
items: Bull has no Shabbat liability → still pays

boxes.title: 👁️ Slave's Eye / Tooth
items: But this is the Torah's special remedy
So bull is exempt, owner-by-self is liable

callout.label: Sharp insight:
callout.text: The Mishnah's mirror-cases are not arbitrary curiosities — they map the precise gap between human moral agency (capital, manumission) and the bull as mere mazik.

lines.speaker: Ben P
lines.text: כָּל הַמְקַלְקְלִין פְּטוּרִין חוּץ מִן הַמַּבְעִיר וְהָעוֹשֶׂה חַבּוּרָה — destructive Shabbat acts don't generate Shabbat liability, except kindling fire and wounding.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: R' Yochanan refines: the מבעיר is liable only עַד שֶׁיְּהֵא צָרִיךְ לָאֶפֶר — when he needs the ash. The wounder is liable only when he needs the blood.

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: Difficulty: but the Mishnah said the bull who burned the stack is liable! Did it need the ash?

lines.speaker: A
lines.text: שׁוֹרוֹ צָרִיךְ אֶת הָאֶפֶר — yes, his bull needs the ash. The Mishnah's case fits the requirement, so the owner pays for tort even though the burning occurred on Shabbat.

callout.label: The chain:
callout.text: Shabbat capital liability requires a constructive act — מְלֶאכֶת מַחְשֶׁבֶת. Pure destruction is exempt unless it serves a productive end (ash, blood).

slides.title: 🐂 The Serial Gorer — R' Meir's Rule
slides.subtitle: How a bull's value gets distributed across multiple victims.

nodes.content: One bull gores 4 or 5 bulls in sequence
יְשַׁלֵּם לָאַחֲרוֹן שֶׁבָּהֶן — pay the last victim first.

columns.label: Still leftover
columns.content: Reverts to the one before that

nodes.content: R' Shimon disputes: with two equal bulls and worthless carcass, each owner takes a מנה — value-based split, not seniority.

callout.label: Logic of seniority:
callout.text: By the time the bull gored the second victim, the first victim already had a lien on the bull's value. R' Meir reverses the intuition: each new attack creates a fresh lien, and the most recent claim collects from a now-encumbered asset.

slides.title: 🧠 Putting the Daf Together
slides.subtitle: Why the Mishnah's mirror-list opens onto Ben Pedaya and the serial gorer.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Mishnah lists asymmetric liability cases: ox vs. person.
2️⃣ Underlying engine: capital/special penalties exempt the human from money.
3️⃣ Shabbat case prompts Ben Pedaya's broader principle on מקלקלין.
4️⃣ R' Yochanan refines: מבעיר and חובל only count if there's a constructive purpose.
5️⃣ The chapter pivots to multi-victim damages → R' Meir's הָאַחֲרוֹן אַחֲרוֹן נִשְׂכָּר.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: The daf maps the seams in liability — between agent and animal, between destruction and creation, between earlier and later claimants on the same damaged asset.

summary.principle: An animal owner pays simple tort whenever his bull damages. A human pays only when no greater penalty (capital, manumission, Shabbat melakhah) absorbs the act. When a single bull damages many, the last victim collects first.

flowSteps.content: Is the actor a person, and does the act carry a greater penalty?

🎓 Key Principle:
Tort liability is structural — it tracks the agent, the act, and the priority of claims on the damaging asset.

quiz.q: Why is the owner exempt when his ox shames someone?
o: Because the ox didn't mean it
Because there is no בּוֹשֶׁת for damage caused by an animal
Because shaming is not a real damage
Because the Mishnah forgot that case
quiz.e: שׁוֹרוֹ שֶׁבִּיֵּישׁ פָּטוּר — the תשלומי בושת payment requires a human agent.

quiz.q: Why is a person exempt for setting fire to a stack on Shabbat?
o: Because fire damage is always exempt
מִפְּנֵי שֶׁנִּידּוֹן בְּנַפְשׁוֹ — the act is capital, so the money obligation is absorbed
Because Shabbat overrides damages
Because he didn't intend the fire
quiz.e: Capital liability blocks monetary liability — קַם לֵיהּ בְּדְרַבָּה מִינֵּיהּ.

quiz.q: What is Ben Pedaya's general principle about destruction on Shabbat?
o: All destruction is liable
כָּל הַמְקַלְקְלִין פְּטוּרִין חוּץ מִן הַמַּבְעִיר וְהָעוֹשֶׂה חַבּוּרָה
Only burning is exempt
Wounding is always exempt
quiz.e: Pure destruction does not constitute melakhah, except kindling and wounding — both of which the Yerushalmi requires to have a productive purpose.

quiz.q: How does R' Yochanan resolve the Mishnah's bull-burning-stack case with his rule?
o: He rejects the Mishnah
He says fire damage is special
שׁוֹרוֹ צָרִיךְ אֶת הָאֶפֶר — the bull's owner needed the ash, satisfying R' Yochanan's condition
He says the case isn't really on Shabbat
quiz.e: R' Yochanan's stipulation that the מבעיר must need the ash is met because the bull's owner did need it.

quiz.q: What is R' Meir's rule for a bull that gores multiple bulls in sequence?
o: Pay all victims equally
הָאַחֲרוֹן אַחֲרוֹן נִשְׂכָּר — the last victim collects first, then leftovers revert backward
Only the first victim is paid
All victims must sue together in one trial
quiz.e: R' Meir reads the Torah as giving the most recent victim priority, with surplus reverting to earlier victims.
